purple.description: Multi-component requirements, signal integration, convergence points

yellow.description: Decision points with alternative pathways (yes/no branching)

description: Sigma factor competition is a fundamental mechanism of global transcriptional regulation in E. coli, where multiple sigma factors compete for binding to a limited pool of RNA polymerase (RNAP) core enzyme. The primary sigma factor σ70 (RpoD) directs housekeeping gene expression, while alternative sigma factors (σ38/RpoS for stationary phase, σ32/RpoH for heat shock, σ54/RpoN for nitrogen metabolism, σ28/FliA for flagellar genes, σ24/RpoE for envelope stress, σ19/FecI for iron transport) redirect transcription in response to specific environmental conditions. Competition is modulated by sigma factor abundance, anti-sigma factors, and core enzyme availability, creating a sophisticated system for coordinating cellular responses to environmental changes.

name: Sigma Factor Competition and Global Transcriptional Control
notes: This flowchart represents the sigma factor competition system with 62 nodes showing environmental sensing, sigma factor synthesis, anti-sigma regulation, competition for core RNAP, holoenzyme formation, and transcriptional program activation. Contains 9 OR logic gates (growth phase detection, stress type identification, anti-sigma regulation for σ70/σ32/σ24, core availability, sigma selection, promoter recognition, program activation, condition monitoring) and 1 AND logic gate (transcription initiation requires holoenzyme AND cognate promoter). The model includes all 7 E. coli sigma factors (σ70, σ38, σ32, σ54, σ28, σ24, σ19), their specific stress responses, anti-sigma factor regulation (DnaK/DnaJ for σ32, RseA for σ24), and the resulting transcriptional programs. Demonstrates how bacteria use sigma factor competition as a computational mechanism for global transcriptional reprogramming in response to environmental changes.
scientificAccuracy: This flowchart is based on extensive genetic, biochemical, and single-molecule studies of sigma factor dynamics in E. coli. The competition model represents current understanding of how sigma factor levels, anti-sigma factor regulation, and core RNAP availability determine transcriptional output. All sigma factors, anti-sigma factors, and regulatory mechanisms have been validated against primary research sources. Logic gates represent actual decision points in sigma factor selection and transcriptional program determination.
